coredata.dc:title: Visible-light photocatalytic O2-to-H2O2 via nitrogen-graphene quantum dots-modified Bi2Fe4O9 for synchronizing the reduction of Cr(VI) and oxidation of organic contaminants: Kinetics, mechanism, and performance
coredata.dc:creator: Wei, Ling-Wei
Zheng, Meng-Wei
Liu, Shou-Heng
Paul Wang, Hong
Pu, Ying-Chih
Nguyen, Van-Can
coredata.dc:description:
Synergistic coexistence of toxic chromium and organic contaminants in wastewater has made treatments highly complicated. As solar-driven wastewater treatments become increasingly environmentally and economically attractive, a photocatalyst (denoted as N-GQDs/Bi2Fe4O9) is developed for photocatalytic reduction of Cr(VI) and simultaneous oxidation of organic contaminants in wastewater. Incorporating N-GQDs (as electron reservoir) into Bi2Fe4O9 modulates the band structure (from 2.40 to 2.24 eV), facilitating charge transfer and electron accumulation at N-GQDs. Due to the photo-Fenton synergistic reaction between N-GQDs/Bi2Fe4O9 photocatalysis process, N-GQDs/ Bi2Fe4O9 demonstrates excellent H2O2 generation (1.64 μM min−1) and H2O2 activation (0.0063 min−1) performance, leading to generation of more highly reactive species (i.e.,
OH radicals), achieving BPA removal of 99.9 % under visible-light irradiation for 100 min. Interestingly, the photo-Fenton synergistic reaction enhances the reduction of Cr(VI) (99.8 %), while the Fe(II) active sites on the N-GQDs/Bi2Fe4O9 can also promote the synchronized Cr(VI) reduction. Also, the accumulated electron around N-GQDs enhances redox cycles of Fe(II)/Fe(III) reactions to realize the sustainable photo-Fenton catalytic reaction. This new N-GQDs/Bi2Fe4O9 photocatalyst demonstrates the feasibility of concurrent reduction of toxic Cr(VI)-to-Cr(III) and oxidation of organic contaminants in wastewater with a simple process via nearly unlimited solar energy.

head.ce:title: Visible-light photocatalytic O2-to-H2O2 via nitrogen-graphene quantum dots-modified Bi2Fe4O9 for synchronizing the reduction of Cr(VI) and oxidation of organic contaminants: Kinetics, mechanism, and performance

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
Synergistic coexistence of toxic chromium and organic contaminants in wastewater has made treatments highly complicated. As solar-driven wastewater treatments become increasingly environmentally and economically attractive, a photocatalyst (denoted as N-GQDs/Bi2Fe4O9) is developed for photocatalytic reduction of Cr(VI) and simultaneous oxidation of organic contaminants in wastewater. Incorporating N-GQDs (as electron reservoir) into Bi2Fe4O9 modulates the band structure (from 2.40 to 2.24 eV), facilitating charge transfer and electron accumulation at N-GQDs. Due to the photo-Fenton synergistic reaction between N-GQDs/Bi2Fe4O9 photocatalysis process, N-GQDs/ Bi2Fe4O9 demonstrates excellent H2O2 generation (1.64 μM min−1) and H2O2 activation (0.0063 min−1) performance, leading to generation of more highly reactive species (i.e.,
OH radicals), achieving BPA removal of 99.9 % under visible-light irradiation for 100 min. Interestingly, the photo-Fenton synergistic reaction enhances the reduction of Cr(VI) (99.8 %), while the Fe(II) active sites on the N-GQDs/Bi2Fe4O9 can also promote the synchronized Cr(VI) reduction. Also, the accumulated electron around N-GQDs enhances redox cycles of Fe(II)/Fe(III) reactions to realize the sustainable photo-Fenton catalytic reaction. This new N-GQDs/Bi2Fe4O9 photocatalyst demonstrates the feasibility of concurrent reduction of toxic Cr(VI)-to-Cr(III) and oxidation of organic contaminants in wastewater with a simple process via nearly unlimited solar energy.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Hexavalent chromium (Cr(VI)), a mutagenic and carcinogenic contaminant, is frequently found in wastewater streams from leather tanneries, printing ink, and metallurgy processes [1]. It is exists in various forms including chromate (CrO4 2−), dichromate (Cr2O7 2−), chromic acid (HCrO4 −) and Cr(III) cations [2]. In particular, removing Cr(VI) compounds such as CrO4 2−, Cr2O7 2−, and CrO4 2− in water is challenging due to their high solubility, with their toxicity being approximately 100 times greater than that of Cr(III) [3]. World Health Organization (WHO) guidelines have strict regulations to control the discharge of Cr(VI) concentrations (<0.05 mg/L) in wastewater [4]. In addition to inorganic contaminants, organic contaminants are frequently found in wastewater. For example, polymerization inhibitor (e.g., bisphenol A (BPA)) often coexists in chromium-contaminated wastewater. BPA is an endocrine-disrupting chemical that poses several negative health effects, including cancer and reproductive disorders [5,6]. When chromium and organic contaminants coexist, their synergistic effects complicate wastewater treatment because of serval factors such as low selectivity for simultaneous reduction and oxidation of inorganic and organic contaminants [7]. Thus, the development of highly efficient and environmentally friendly methods for the simultaneous treatment of inorganic and organic contaminants has attracted great attention.

Currently, various treatments address chromium-contaminated wastewater, including reduction, flocculation, and microbial reduction. While effective, they suffer from high costs, low recovery, and poor organic contaminant oxidation [8–10]. Photocatalysis offers an environmentally and economically attractive alternative [11]. In the photocatalysis process, photocatalysts generate e− and h+ for Cr(VI) reduction and organic contaminant oxidation, while also promoting the conversion of O2 to reactive oxygen species (ROS) for the effective oxidation of organic contaminants and reduction of heavy metals [12]. These ROS, including superoxide (
O2 −), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), and hydroxyl (
OH) radicals, are generally generated by the Fenton reaction [13]. These ROS exhibit robust oxidation performances, thereby further promoting the oxidation efficiency of organic contaminants by demethylation and hydroxylation. However, in traditional Fenton reactions, the cycling reaction rate of Fe(II)/Fe(III) (2 × 10−3 M−1s−1) is much lower than that of Fenton reaction (63 M−1s−1) [14], leading to low efficiency in H2O2 activation and the oxidation of organic contaminants. Synchronizing photocatalysis and the Fenton reaction (photo-Fenton reaction) can effectively transfer the Fe valence state by photogenerated electrons (Fe3+/Fe2+, +0.77 V vs. NHE), potentially accelerating the cycle of Fe(III)/Fe(II) to enhance Fenton reaction [15]. It is noteworthy that Fe(II) tends to donate electrons, which contributes to the reduction of Cr(VI) to less toxic Cr(III) [15]. Unfortunately, the photo-Fenton synergy method is still far from the necessity of engineering applications due to low charge transfer and separation efficiency. Consequently, the design of an efficient photocatalyst plays a pivotal in improving the separation efficiency of charge carriers and facilitating in-situ utilization of ROS [16].

Bismuth ferrite (Bi2Fe4O9), a multiferroic semiconductor, is a promising candidate photocatalyst for photocatalytic oxidation reactions, owing to its electronic, dielectric, and chemical stability [17]. The mullite-type Bi2Fe4O9 possesses a unique layered structure composed of Bi4O4 hexahedra slabs, FeO6 octahedra layers, and FeO4 tetrahedra layers [18]. This structural arrangement has a unique internal electric field, which can facilitate the effective migration of photogenerated free charges for the interconversion of the Fe valence state [19]. Additionally, due to its narrow bandgap energy, Bi2Fe4O9 exhibits steady and rapid photocurrent responses under visible-light irradiation [20]. Nevertheless, Bi2Fe4O9 lacks sufficient active sites, which renders the photocatalytic conversion of H2O to H2O2 thermodynamically unfavorable [21]. Thus, modifying Bi2Fe4O9 with active species, such as graphitic, pyrrolic, and pyridinic species, can improve the efficiency of H2O2 generation.

Nitrogen-graphene quantum dots (GQDs), quasi-zero-dimension (0 D) nanoparticles with sizes ranging from 2 to 10 nm, exhibit excellent properties such as low cost, high stability, electric conductivity, and unique edge effects [22–24]. Interestingly, N-GQDs not only adjust the energy edge of semiconductors and improve visible-light absorption but also provide superior electron storage and transfer performance due to their inherent conjugated π structure, thereby facilitating charge dissociation and migration [25,26]. Particularly, specific nitrogen species on the N-GQDs have pyrrolic-N and pyridinic sites, which can promote O2 activation to H2O2 (O2 →
O2 − → H2O2) [27,28]. This inspired design and synthesis of new nitrogen-doped GQDs/Bi2Fe4O9 composite (N-GDQs/Bi2Fe4O9) for photocatalytic reduction of Cr(VI) and oxidation of organic contaminants simultaneously in wastewater. In this work, N-GQDs synthesized by a facile molten salt impregnation method were doped onto Bi2Fe4O9 to improve photogenerated charge migration and separation for enhanced photocatalytic activity. In addition, N-GQDs/Bi2Fe4O9, a critical photocatalyst, was employed in a synergistic photocatalytic process involving photocatalytic O2-to-H2O2, in situ H2O2 activation, Cr reduction, and oxidation of organic contaminants (i.e., BPA). To demonstrate engineering applications of the N-GQDs/Bi2Fe4O9 photocatalysts, their reaction kinetics for photocatalytic generation of H2O2, reduction of Cr(VI)-to-Cr(III) and oxidation of BPA were investigated. Also, the loading of the N-GQDs/Bi2Fe4O9 photocatalyst into a thin photocatalysis reactor demonstrated its feasibility for engineering applications, achieving the nearly complete reduction of Cr(VI) and degradation of organic contaminants from simulated wastewater.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of the N-GQDs and GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: For preparation of N-GQDs, triammonium citrate (0.03 mol) (99.5 %, Sigma-Aldrich) was dissolved in the deionized water (25 mL) and then stirred for 1 h. The solution was transferred to the Teflon-lined autoclave (50 mL) and heated at 473 K for 30 min to form a dark orange solution. The obtained solution was adjusted to pH 6.0 by using NaOH solution (0.25 M) (98 %, Sigma-Aldrich) due to the high emission intensity observed within the pH range of 5–6 [22]. The N-GQDs solution was then dialyzed (Biotech CE dialysis trial kit, 500–1000 Da) for 24 h and stored at 277 K. For comparison, the citric acid (0.03 mol) (99.5 %, Sigma-Aldrich) was used as precursors to prepare GQDs via the above method.

The Bi2Fe4O9 photocatalyst was synthesized via a hydrothermal method. Briefly, Bi(NO3)3·5H2O (2 mmol) (98 %, Sigma-Aldrich) and Fe(NO)3·9H2O (2 mmol) (98 %, J.T. Baker) were dissolved in HNO3 (1 M, 7.5 mL) (65 %, Sigma-Aldrich) and stirred for 30 min to obtain a homogeneous solution. The solution was transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave (50 mL) and heated at 473 K for 24 h. The resulting solution was centrifugated at 7000 rpm and washed with ultrapure water/ethanol. The obtained Bi2Fe4O9 photocatalyst was then dried at 333 K for 8 h.

The N-GQDs/Bi2Fe4O9 photocatalyst was prepared via a facile hydrothermal method. Briefly, Bi(NO3)3·5H2O (2 mmol) (98 %, Sigma-Aldrich) and Fe(NO)3·9H2O (2 mmol) (98 %, J.T. Baker) were dissolved in HNO3 (1 M, 7.5 mL) (65 %, Sigma-Aldrich), which was well mixed with the N-GQDs solution for 30 min to obtain a homogeneous solution. The mixture was transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave (50 mL) and heated at 473 K for 24 h. Upon centrifugation at 7000 rpm, the N-GQDs/Bi2Fe4O9 photocatalysts were washed with ultrapure water/ethanol and dried at 333 K for 8 h. Likewise, the GQDs/Bi2Fe4O9 photocatalysts were also prepared with the similar procedures.

Morphologies of the photocatalysts were determined by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEOL JEM-2100F). The crystalline structures were studied by X-ray diffraction (XRD, Bruker D8 Advance) with a CuKα radiation at a scan rate of 3° min−1 from 20 to 80° (2θ). The function groups of photocatalysts were measured by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, Thermo, NICOLET 6700) between 500 and 4000 cm−1 at a resolution of 4 cm−1. The quantified content of N-GQDs was measured by the thermogravimetric analyzer (TGA, TA instrument, SDT 650). Absorption spectra of the photocatalysts were also obtained via a diffuse reflectance UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Varian Cary 100) with an integrating sphere apparatus using BaSO4 as a reference. The charge carrier separation rates were determined by photoluminescence (PL, Hitachi, F-700, λex = 375 nm) and time-resolved photoluminescence (TRPL, Pico Quant PDL 800-B, GaN diode laser, λex = 375 nm) spectroscopies. The BPA intermediates (water and methanol as mobile phase) in negative ion mode within the range of 60–300 m/z were analyzed by liquid chromatography-mass spectrometer (LC-MS, Thermo Fisher Scientific Q-Exactive plus) with a flow rate of 0.35 mL min−1.

Surface chemical structure of the photocatalysts was investigated by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, VG scientific, ESCALAB-250) equipped with an Al Ka X-ray source. At the pressures below 1 × 10−8 Torr, The X-ray beam was incident on the hemispherical detector at 45° relative to the sample stage. Fresh and used photocatalysts were dropped on the Cu tape and dried at 303 K under an argon atmosphere for 24 h. High-resolution XPS spectra of C 1s, N 1s, Bi 4f, Fe 2p, and O 1 s were collected with 80 sweeps per element at an energy resolution of 220 ± 4 eV.

A three-electrode system on an electrochemical workstation (CHI Instruments 727D) was used to study photoelectrochemical properties of the photocatalysts. The working electrodes were made by slow drop-coating of a slurry containing the photocatalysts (20 mg), ethanol (200 μL), and Nafion solution (20 μL) onto FTO electrodes (20 × 20 mm2). The Na2SO4 (0.5 M), Ag/AgCl, and Pt were used as electrolytes, reference, and counter electrodes, respectively. The electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) of the photocatalysts were determined in the frequency range of 10−1 to 105 Hz. The Mott-Schottky (MS) plots were measured at 0.5, 1.0, and 2.0 kHz. Also, under the Xe arc lamp irradiation equipped with a monochromatic cut-off filter at 420 nm, photocurrent densities of the photocatalysts were measured at 0 V under on and off visible-light irradiation for five cycles. The electron-transfer number was measured using a Pine AFMSRXE system. The slurry solution (20 μL) was deposited onto a disk electrode to serve as the working electrode. The linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) was conducted in an O2-saturated Na2SO4 electrolyte (0.5 M) at various rotating speeds (200, 400, 600, 800, 1000, and 1200 rpm). The average numbers of electron transfer (n) were then calculated using the Kentucky–Levich equation (Eq. (1)).

where j is the measured current (mA cm−2), jk is the kinetic limiting current (mA−1 cm−2), F is the Faraday constant (96485C mol−1), D is the oxygen diffusion coefficient (1.93 × 10−5 cm2 s−1), ν is the viscosity (0.01 cm2 s−1), C is the saturated oxygen concentration in water (1.26 × 10−3 M), and ω is the electrode rotation rate (rpm).

The photocatalytic experiments were conducted in a recirculating photocatalysis system (see Fig. S1) (volume: 500 cm3, dimension: 150 mm (L) x 130 mm (W) x 70 mm (thickness)). The photocatalysts were loaded onto a thin film of glass in the reactor. Potassium dichromate (K2Cr2O7) (99.8 %, Sigma-Aldrich) and bisphenol A (BPA) (≥99 %, Sigma-Aldrich) were selected as representative contaminants. Prior to photocatalysis, the simulated wastewater (100 mL) containing photocatalysts (0.5–1.8 mg L−1), Cr(VI) (20 mg L−1), and BPA (5–25 mg L−1) was purged with high-purity oxygen gas and stirred in the dark for 30 min at 298 K. The feed flow rate was controlled using a peristaltic pump within the range of 0.03–0.11 mL s−1. A 500 W Xenon arc lamp (Newport) with a light cut-off (SP, CM 100) was used for the photocatalysis experiments. The solution was then passed and collected through a PTFE filter (0.22 μm) at defined time intervals.

For the determined Cr(VI) concentrations, 1,5-diphenylcarbazide (DPC) (0.02 M, 0.3 mL) (Sigma-Aldrich), H2SO4 (0.2 M, 0.2 mL), and deionized water (7 mL) were added into the wastewater (0.5 mL) for UV–Vis spectroscopic analysis (at 540 nm) of the DPC-Cr(VI) complex. Also, BPA concentrations were determined by high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC, Thermo Dionex Ultimate 3000) equipped with a C18 column (methanol: deionized water = 75:25) and a UV detector at 275 nm. According to the pseudo-first-order model, the reaction kinetics was calculated by the equation (Eq. (2)).
(2)

= k t
where C0 and C denote the contaminants concentrations at initial time and reaction time, respectively; the k denotes the kinetic rate constant.

In a recirculating photocatalysis system (see Fig. S1) with a 500 W Xenon arc lamp (cut off 400 nm), the O2-saturated aqueous solution was fed flow into the photocatalytic system for photocatalytic O2-to-H2O2 experiments. In the photocatalytic processes, concentrations of H2O2 were measured using the DMP-Cu method by UV–Vis spectroscopy (Varian, Carry 100) at 454 nm. Typically, the sample (0.5 mL) was mixed with 2,9-dimethyl-1,10-phenanthroline (10 mg mL−1, 0.5 mL) (DMP, 98 %, Alfa Aesar), copper(II) sulfate (0.01 M, 0.5 mL) (99 %, Sigma-Aldrich) and phosphate buffer solution (0.1 M, 0.5 mL) (Sigma-Aldrich, pH = 7.4) for UV/Vis spectroscopic analysis studies [29].

A simulated wastewater containing photocatalysts, BPA, Cr(VI), and scavengers was prepared and sonicated for 10 min to achieve homogeneity. To determine the contribution of the ROS to the contaminants in the photocatalytic reactions, scavengers such as ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid disodium (EDTA-2Na) (1 mM), 1,4-benzoquinone (BQ) (1 mM), and isopropanol (IPA) (10 mM) were employed as quenchers for h+,
O2 −, and
OH radicals, respectively. Additionally, 5,5-dimethyl-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) was used as a trapping reagent. Subsequently, 0.2 mL of the mixture solution was transferred to the resonant cavity of an electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectrometer (Bruker EMX-P). Under visible-light irradiation, the DMPO-
O2 − and DMPO-
OH species were employed to confirm the generation of highly reactive species.

Density functional theory (DFT) calculations for photocatalysts (e.g., Bi2Fe4O9 and N-GQDs) were performed using the Cambridge Sequential Total Energy Package (CASTEP). The plane-wave energy cutoff is set at 400 eV, the convergence-energy criterion is 10−5 eV, and forces of 0.01 eV/Å are utilized for the projector augmented wave, which describes the electron–ion interaction. A Monkhorst-Packing grid (1 × 1 × 1) and denser k-points (3 × 3 × 1) are employed to compute electrical performances. Theoretical calculations of the frontier molecular orbitals for the organic contaminant (i.e., BPA), including structure and geometry optimizations and energy point simulation on the DFT, were performed using Materials Studio (DMol3/B3LYP code). For all calculations, the generalized gradient approximation (GGA) within the Perdew-Burke-Ernzerhof (PBE) function was employed to account for the exchange–correlation effects of BPA. Additionally, using the conjugate gradient algorithm, the geometry optimization convergence was set up, i.e., 1.0 × 10−5 eV Å−1 for force tolerance and 0.004 Ha Å−1 for the maximum force on the atom. Fukui functions were calculated by Multiwfn software. Simulations of the intermediates in the BPA oxidation pathways were performed using the toxicity estimating software and quantitative structure–activity relationships (QSAR).

Crystalline structures of the Bi2Fe4O9, GQDs/Bi2Fe4O9, and N-GQDs/Bi2Fe4O9 photocatalysts are shown in Fig. 1 (A). The Bi2Fe4O9 has main diffraction peaks at 14.5°, 26.5°, 27.9°, 32.8°, 46.8°, 50.1°, 52.3°, 56.5°, 62.4°, 65.2°, and 67.3°, which are ascribed to the (001), (121), (211), (220), (141), (411), (123), (322), (004), (342), and (530) planes, respectively (JCPDS No. 25–0090). Compared with Bi2Fe4O9, N-GQDs/Bi2Fe4O9 has similar characteristic peaks, and no new peaks emerge, suggesting that the incorporation of N-GQDs into Bi2Fe4O9 may not cause significant changes in the Bi2Fe4O9 crystalline structure. This result may also be attributed to the lower concentrations and relatively lower crystallinity of N-GQDs compared to Bi2Fe4O9, as well as their high dispersion within the material surface [30,31]. Moreover, in the XRD patterns (see Fig. S2), no evident shifts can be observed in the (211) and (220) planes, again confirming that the incorporation of N-GQDs may not lead to the distortion of Bi2Fe4O9 structure.

Functional groups of the photocatalysts were also studied by FTIR (Fig. 1(B)). The GQDs/Bi2Fe4O9 and N-GQDs/Bi2Fe4O9 have similar IR spectra. The characteristic features at 1410 and 3200–3500 cm−1 are related to the O-H bending and O-H stretching, suggesting that the surface has highly hydrophilic properties. The feature at 1675 cm−1 is attributed to CO stretching. The N-GQDs/Bi2Fe4O9 has additional features at 1495 cm−1 that may be related to the N-H stretching. The strong features at 550 and 810 cm−1 are associated with the vibrations of O atoms in the FeO6 and the Fe-O vibrations in the FeO4, respectively [32,33]. As shown in Fig. 1(C), the content of N-GQDs was identified by TGA. Two stages of distinct weight loss were observed. The initial weight loss (ca. 0.2 %) observed at 343–543 K is attributed to the desorption of surface-absorbed water. Subsequently, as the temperature rises from 630 to 950 K, the N-GQDs undergo decomposition and conversion into gaseous-phase compounds. Compared with Bi2Fe4O9, the amount of N-GQDs in the N-GQDs/Bi2Fe4O9 can be estimated to be 4.3 %.

Morphologies of the NGQDs/Bi2Fe4O9 photocatalysts have been determined by high-resolution TEM (HR-TEM). In Fig. 1(D), the size of N-GQDs/Bi2Fe4O9 is ca. 150 nm. The presence of N-GQDs can also be observed in Fig. (E). The clear lattice spacing of Bi2Fe4O9 is 0.32 nm, corresponding to the (121) plane of the Bi2Fe4O9 crystal (Fig. 1(F)), which is consistent with the XRD results. Meanwhile, in the HR-TEM image of N-GQDs (Fig. 1(G)), the lattice spacing of 0.21 nm is indexed to the (100) lattice plane of graphitic carbon [34]. Fig. 1(H) reveals the high dispersion of N-GQDs on the surface of Bi2Fe4O9 with an average particle diameter of 4.5 ± 2 nm. Furthermore, the elemental mapping (Fig. 1(I)) indicates the distribution of Bi, Fe, N, and C species, confirming the uniform dispersion of N-GQDs onto Bi2Fe4O9.

Chemical structure of Bi2Fe4O9 and N-GQDs/Bi2Fe4O9 photocatalysts were studied by XPS. Fig. 2 (A) shows the presence of the relevant elements such as bismuth (Bi), oxygen (O), iron (Fe), nitrogen (N) and carbon (C) in the photocatalyst, suggesting that the N-GQDs are successfully incorporated into the Bi2Fe4O9. In Fig. 2(B), the C 1 s species are deconvoluted to four peaks centered at 284.7, 285.4, 286.3 and 288.4 eV, corresponding to C-C, C-N, C-O, and O-CO species, respectively. The existence of pyridinic-N (396.9 eV), pyrrolic-N (399.6 eV), and graphitic-N (402.1 eV) can be observed by the deconvoluted N 1 s spectra (Fig. 2(C)) [35]. Accordingly, these results indicate that pyrrolic-N (45.1 %) and graphitic-N (41.1 %) species are the main nitrogen species in the Bi2Fe4O9 photocatalyst. Importantly, the pyrrolic-N and pyridinic-N as active sites exhibit a robust electron-rich effect and establish strong interactions with O2 [36]. This suggests that by incorporating the N-GQDs into Bi2Fe4O9, the electron density can be enhanced to a certain extent [35]. In the Bi spectrum (Fig. 2(D)), the deconvoluted Bi 4f bands centered at 158 and 163 eV are ascribed to Bi 4f5/2 and Bi 4f7/2 of Bi(III) species. Also, the coexistence of Fe(II) (710 and 723 eV) and Fe(III) (712 and 726 eV) species are identified on the N-GQDs/Bi2Fe4O9 (Fig. 2(E)). In the O 1 s XPS spectra (Fig. 2(F)), the deconvoluted O 1 s species have the binding energy of 529, 531, and 532 eV, corresponding to lattice oxygen, oxygen vacancies, and adsorbed oxygen, respectively. Compared to the Bi2Fe4O9, the peaks corresponding to Bi, Fe, and O in N-GQDs/Bi2Fe4O9 are shifted to higher energies, suggesting the chemical interactions between N-GQDs and Bi2Fe4O9. Also, the existence of strong interaction between N-GQDs and Bi2Fe4O9 may accelerate charge migration from Bi2Fe4O9 to N-GQDs, thereby promoting the efficient charge separation.

To investigate the up-conversion luminescence effect of the GQDs and N-GQDs, the up-converted PL spectra were measured in the range of 450 to 650 nm. In Fig. S3, N-GQDs exhibit strong emitting light at 550 nm. Additionally, compared to the GQDs, the N-GQDs show a significant red-shift in the wavelength emission of 500–600 nm, suggesting that the multiple photons can be expanded to the visible-light region, thereby improving the efficiency of solar-light conversion. This observed effect of N-GQDs can be attributed to the fluorescence process arising from π-π electron transition within conjugated π structures [23], which leads to charge delocalization and a reduction in the bandgap, facilitating charge transfer and excitation.

Fig. 3 (A) shows the UV/Vis diffuse spectra of the Bi2Fe4O9, GQDs/Bi2Fe4O9, and N-GQDs/Bi2Fe4O9 photocatalysts. The pristine Bi2Fe4O9 exhibits an absorption edge at 516 nm. In the presence of GQDs, the absorption edge is shifted to 548 nm, and this shift is further extended to 553 nm with the incorporation of nitrogen into GQDs on the Bi2Fe4O9. In addition, the N-GQDs/Bi2Fe4O9 photocatalyst has a relatively high absorbance in the visible-light region, which may be attributed to the N-GQDs serving as sensitizers and photocenters to enhance visible light absorption capability [37]. By employing the Kubelka-Munk formula, the determined bandgap energies for the Bi2Fe4O9, GQDs/Bi2Fe4O9, and N-GQDs/Bi2Fe4O9 photocatalysts are 2.40, 2.26, and 2.24 eV, respectively (see Fig. 3(B)). The decreasing bandgap energy may be attributed to changes in localized electronic states and band structure [38]. Additionally, the interfacial charge transfer reaction between N-GQDs and Bi2Fe4O9 also contributes to this phenomenon. This implies that the introduction of N-GQDs to Bi2Fe4O9 can promote the separation of electrons and holes, thereby enhancing photocatalytic performance.

Separation efficiency and lifetime of photogenerated charge carriers for the photocatalysts have been studied by PL and TRPL using an excitation wavelength (375 nm). Fig. 3(C) shows that the N-GQDs/Bi2Fe4O9 exhibits the lowest intensity of the PL band centered at 550 nm among all the photocatalysts, indicating the superior separation rate of photogenerated charges. Moreover, the N-GQDs/Bi2Fe4O9 photocatalyst has an additional band at 590–630 nm, which may be associated with the presence of graphitic-N and pyrrolic-N [39]. As such, the N-GQDs with π-conjugation could serve as charge-trapping sites, resulting in the better efficiency of charge separation than the GQDs which are incorporated into Bi2Fe4O9 [40]. Photogenerated charge transfer kinetics of the photocatalysts have been determined by TRPL (Fig. 3(D)). The TRPL data were fitted with the double-exponential decay functions to resolve the radiative (τ1) and non-radiative transitions (τ2) decay times. As observed in Table S1, the N-GQDs/Bi2Fe4O9 (2.93 ns) has the smaller average decay time (τav) than the Bi2Fe4O9 (3.64 ns) and GQDs/Bi2Fe4O9 (3.31 ns), implying that the presence of N-GQDs can enhance the charge transfer. N-GQDs/Bi2Fe4O9 primarily follows the τ2 transfer routes of the photogenerated charge carriers and exhibits a high prolonged charge lifetime. These results suggest that N-GQDs/Bi2Fe4O9 achieves the higher efficiency in the charge separation, which may be attributed to the presence of unpaired electrons in the π-bonded clusters of N-GQDs [41]. Moreover, the N-GQDs with the extended π-conjugation of pyrrolic-N can enhance electron delocalization and reduce the resistance of electron transportation as compared with GQDs.

Electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) of the Bi2Fe4O9, GQDs/Bi2Fe4O9, and N-GQDs/Bi2Fe4O9 photocatalysts were used to reveal their charge transfer performances. Fig. 4 (A) displays the charge-transfer resistance (Rct) and equivalent resistance observed in the high-frequency region. Compared with Bi2Fe4O9 and GQDs/Bi2Fe4O9, the N-GQDs/Bi2Fe4O9 photocatalyst exhibits a relatively low semicircle radius and low Rct (see Table S2), again confirming that the presence of N-GQDs enhances the transfer of photogenerated charge carriers, leading to a reduction in charge-carrier resistance. Furthermore, the photocurrent densities of the photocatalysts were studied during on–off irradiation of visible light, as depicted in Fig. 4(B). The N-GQDs/Bi2Fe4O9 (5.8 μA cm−2) has greater photocurrent density associated with the higher separation efficiency of photogenerated charge carriers than Bi2Fe4O9 (1.2 μA cm−2) and GQDs/Bi2Fe4O9 (3.1 μA cm−2) by 1.8–4.8 times, indicating that the modified Bi2Fe4O9 photocatalyst by N-GQDs enhances charge separation and transfer rates, resulting in the enhanced photocurrent response and photocatalytic activity under visible-light irradiation.

Mott-Schottky measurements were conducted at different frequencies (e.g., 0.5, 1.0, and 2.0) to estimate the flat potentials (Efb) of photocatalysts, as described by the equation (Eq. (2)) [42].

where C is interfacial capacitance, ε and ε0 are the dielectric constant of the material and permittivity in a vacuum, respectively, Nd represents the density of charge carriers; q indicates the electronic charge (1.602 × 10−19C), and E, Efb, kb, and T indicate the applied voltage, flat potential, Boltzmann constant, and temperature, respectively.

The Efb of the Bi2Fe4O9, GQDs/Bi2Fe4O9 and N-GQDs/Bi2Fe4O9 are −0.69, −0.79 and −0.84 V vs. NHE, respectively (Fig. 4(C)). Compared with GQDs/Bi2Fe4O9, the N-GQDs/Bi2Fe4O9 exhibits a relatively more negative flat potential, indicating its higher photocatalytic reduction capabilities. In Fig. 4(D), the band-gap energy levels of GQDs/Bi2Fe4O9 and N-GQDs/Bi2Fe4O9 are established by combining the band-gap energy with Efb, obtained from the Tauc and Mott–Schottky plots, respectively. The valance band potentials (Ev) of the Bi2Fe4O9, GQDs/Bi2Fe4O9 and N-GQDs/Bi2Fe4O9 can be calculated to 1.93, 1.65, and 1.46 eV, respectively. These results indicate that the incorporation of N-GQDs onto Bi2Fe4O9 causes a negative shift of the conduction band (Ec), which may be attributed to the effects of state density distribution changes on the band structure. In Fig. 4(D), the bandgap energy potentials of N-GQDs/Bi2Fe4O9 for Cr(VI)/Cr(V) (+0.6 V vs. NHE), O2 activation (−0.33 V vs. NHE), H2O2 generation (+0.68 V vs. NHE), and Fe cyclic reactions (+0.77 V vs. NHE) are suitable, indicating that it is thermodynamically feasible for the simultaneous photocatalytic reduction of Cr(VI) and organic contaminants. Additionally, N-GQDs/Bi2Fe4O9 may be involved in the continuous two-electron or one-electron pathways for O2 reduction (O2 + e− →
O2 − + e− + 2H+ → H2O2) and Cr(VI) reduction.

In general, the doping N atom into GQDs can induce charge delocalization, leading to changes in the band structure and affecting the work functions (ϕ) [43]. Using the DFT calculations, the values of ϕ (i.e., distances between the Fermi energy level and vacuum energy level) observed for N-GQDs and Bi2Fe4O9 are estimated to be 4.97 and 4.13 V vs. vacuum, respectively (Fig. 4(E)). These results suggest that the photogenerated electron can rapidly transfer from Bi2Fe4O9 to N-GQDs due to these differences in work functions. Fig. 4(F) shows the charge flow mechanism of N-GQDs/Bi2Fe4O9. Typically, the charge separation rate in Bi2Fe4O9 is unfavorable for photocatalytic efficiency. To improve effectiveness, a rapid charge transfer rate is needed for charge separation by modifying Bi2Fe4O9 with N-GQDs. When the Bi2Fe4O9 contacts N-GQDs, the energy band of Bi2Fe4O9 undergoes downward bending due to the higher Fermi level of N-GQDs compared to Bi2Fe4O9, aligning the Fermi levels to equilibrium and forming the stable internal electric field (see Fig. 4(F)). Subsequently, under visible-light irradiation, photogenerated electrons are excited and accumulated at the Ec of N-GQDs, while the formation of heterojunction at the interface hinders electron recombination with photogenerated holes [44], ensuring the maximum redox performances to enhance the photocatalytic reduction activity [45].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synergistic effect between photocatalytic H2O2 generation and Cr(VI) reduction
ce:section.ce:para:
Photocatalytic generation of H2O2 concentration was determined using the DMP-Cu method. Fig. 5 (A) shows the photocatalytic O2-to-H2O2 generation in the absence of Cr(VI)) contaminant by using Bi2Fe4O9, GQDs/Bi2Fe4O9, and N-GQDs/Bi2Fe4O9. Under visible-light irradiation for 135 min, the accumulated concentration of H2O2 generated by N-GQDs/Bi2Fe4O9 is 231.6 μM. Also, the H2O2 formation reaction rate (kf ) follows the zero-order kinetics. A higher yield of H2O2 (kf = 1.64 μM min−1) is observed for N-GQDs/Bi2Fe4O9, surpassing that of Bi2Fe4O9 by more than three times. The high H2O2 generation rate of N-GQDs/Bi2Fe4O9 may be attributed to the N active sites (i.e., pyrrolic-N and pyridinic-N) on N-GQDs. Importantly, the active sites of pyrrolic-N and pyridinic-N for two-electron O2 conversion reactions (O2 →
O2 − → H2O2) possess ideal absorption and desorption energies, which facilitates photocatalytic H2O2 generation activity [46]. This result may be attributed to the N-GQDs/Bi2Fe4O9 towards two-electron transfer pathways for photocatalytic H2O2 generation. Figure S4 (A-C) presents the LSV polarization curves of the photocatalysts obtained at various rotating rates in the O2-saturated system. N-GQDs/Bi2Fe4O9 exhibits a higher onset potential for promoting the oxygen reduction reaction compared to Bi2Fe4O9 and GQDs/Bi2Fe4O9 (Fig. S4(C)). The electron-transfer number (n) in the oxygen reduction reaction was determined using Koutecky–Levich plots [47]. In Fig. S4(D), the n values for Bi2Fe4O9, GQDs/Bi2Fe4O9, and N-GQDs/Bi2Fe4O9 are 1.89, 2.12, and 2.57, respectively. N-GQDs/Bi2Fe4O9 predominantly facilitates the photocatalytic O2-to-H2O2 reduction via the two-electron transfer pathway, indicating its high selectivity for two-electron transfer.

Photo-Fenton activity in visible-light photocatalytic reactions was evaluated through H2O2 decomposition. The reaction kinetics in the photocatalytic H2O2 reaction were calculated by using the equation (Eq. (3)) [48], where kf and kd are the formation and activation rate constants, respectively. The H2O2 activation rate (kd) was also determined using first-order kinetic. Fig. 5(B) shows the activation of H2O2 by the Bi2Fe4O9, GQDs/Bi2Fe4O9, and N-GQDs/Bi2Fe4O9 photocatalysts. Under visible-light irradiation, N-GQDs/Bi2Fe4O9 exhibits a high H2O2 activation rate constant (kd = 0.0063 min−1), which is 1.46 times higher than that of Bi2Fe4O9 (kd = 0.0043 min−1). This difference can potentially be attributed to the influence of excessive electrons consuming H2O2 to facilitate the transition of the Fe valance state [49]. It is worth noting that N-GQDs/Bi2Fe4O9 also has the highest kf constants (see Fig. 5(C)), suggesting that the incorporation of N-GQDs on Bi2Fe4O9 enhances photo-Fenton catalytic performance.

Meanwhile, the yields of H2O2 produced by the Bi2Fe4O9, GQDs/Bi2Fe4O9, and N-GQDs/Bi2Fe4O9 photocatalysts in the simulated wastewater are depicted in Fig. 5(D). Under visible-light irradiation for 120 min, an accumulated H2O2 concentration of 119.4 μM is observed by N-GQDs/Bi2Fe4O9. The H2O2 concentration decreases by 1.9 times compared to the condition without Cr(VI) contaminants. On the other hand, photocatalytic Cr(VI) reduction performances using Bi2Fe4O9, GQDs/Bi2Fe4O9, and N-GQDs/Bi2Fe4O9 photocatalysts were investigated under visible light irradiation (λ > 400 nm). As expected, the rate of Cr(VI) reduction exhibits a similar trend as the H2O2 activation rate. Under visible-light irradiation for 135 min, the efficiency of Cr(VI) conversion by the Bi2Fe4O9 photocatalyst is low (15 %). However, time-dependent photocatalytic reduction of Cr(VI) is significantly increased to 99 % upon using N-GQDs/Bi2Fe4O9 (Fig. 5(E)). Also, time-dependent photocatalytic Cr(VI) reduction reaction follows the pseudo-first-order reaction kinetics, as shown in Fig. 5(F). Accordingly, a significantly higher kinetic rate constant of Cr(VI) reduction (0.023 min−1) is observed for N-GQDs/Bi2Fe4O9, indicating that photocatalytic H2O2 generation and activation can contribute to the synchronized photocatalytic reduction of Cr(VI) reaction. These findings indicate a synergistic effect between the photo-Fenton and Cr(VI) reduction reactions in the N-GQDs/Bi2Fe4O9.

Electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) was utilized to identify the main reactive species in the photo-Fenton synergistic reaction. In Fig. 6 (A), the photocatalytic generation of
O2 − is observed in the photocatalysis processes. N-GQDs/Bi2Fe4O9 shows a strong DMPO-
O2 − signal compared to Bi2Fe4O9 and GQDs/Bi2Fe4O9, indicating the high efficiency of O2-to-
O2 − conversion by N-GQDs. These results correlated with H2O2 generation efficiency are consistent, confirming the presence of the main
O2 − species in the photocatalytic H2O2 generation process. Note that the signal intensity of
O2 − radicals generated in the N-GQDs/Bi2Fe4O9 after visible light irradiation is ca. 3.5 times higher than Bi2Fe4O9 (see Fig. 6(A)). This can be attributed to the incorporation of N-GQDs into Bi2Fe4O9, which facilitates charge separation, leading to more electrons available for the activation of O2 molecules. Similarly, under the photo-Fenton synergistic reaction, the signal of DMPO-
OH from N-GQDs/Bi2Fe4O9 also shows the highest intensity (Fig. 6(B)), further confirming that the electron reservoir performances of N-GQDs with up-converted photoluminescence can improve light utilization for the enhanced generation of active
OH species under the photo-Fenton synergistic reaction. These results again reveal the efficient photocatalytic oxidation of organic contaminants. Importantly, this implies that the photogenerated electron on Bi2Fe4O9 transfers to N-GQDs, enabling reduction of O2 to
O2 − (O2 + e− →
O2 −) and generation of H2O2 (
O2 − + e− + 2H+ → H2O2) through the continuous two-electron transfer pathway. The
OH radical can be formed from H2O2 through subsequent Fenton reaction (Fe(II) + H2O2 → Fe(III) + 6OH− +
OH).

Additionally, the generation of
O2 − and
OH radicals on the N-GQDs/Bi2Fe4O9 were measured by 2,3-bis(2-methoxy-4-nitro-5-sulfophenyl)–2H-tetrazolium-5-carboxanilide (XTT) and Coumarin, respectively (see Text S2 and S3). The increasing adducts peaks of the XTT formazan and 7-hydroxycoumarin with light irradiation time can be observed in Fig. 6(C) and 6(D), which further confirmed that the existence of
O2 − and
OH radicals in photocatalytic reaction. Also, the concentration of
O2 − radicals was measured using the Beer-Lambert, while the concentration of
OH radicals was determined by analyzing the transformation of coumarin to 7-hydroxycoumarin. Under visible light irradiation for 120 min, the
O2 − and
OH radicals are 171.9 and 99.4 μM, respectively (Fig. 6(E)). This result demonstrates a similar trend in the generation of
O2 −, H2O2, and
OH radicals, suggesting that O2 in the photocatalytic process undergoes a series of reactions (O2 + e− →
O2 − + e− + 2H+ → H2O2 + e− →
OH) via the continuous electron transfer pathway.

Further XPS analysis was conducted to examine the interaction of Cr and Fe on the N-GQDs/Bi2Fe4O9 under photocatalytic reactions. Fig. 7 (A) and 7(B) show the chemical state changes of Fe 2p and Cr 2p spectra under visible-light irradiation. Fe(II) (709.5 and 722.4 eV), Fe(III) (712.1 and 724.2 eV), Cr(VI) (580.4 and 589.3 eV), and Cr(III) (577.4 and 586.7 eV) species are identified on the N-GQDs/Bi2Fe4O9. With the increased photocatalysis reaction time (i.e., from 20 to 140 min), the percentage of Fe(II) species is increased from 43.5 to 54.4 %. However, the relative intensities of Cr(VI) species are decreased to 6.9 %, suggesting a possible interaction between Fe and Cr [50]. In addition, the deconvoluted Cr 2p species (see Fig. 7(B)) have binding energies of 578.7 and 587.6 eV, corresponding to Cr(V) species. Under visible-light irradiation, relatively increased areas of Cr(V) are observed, suggesting the formation of intermediate Cr(V) species during the Cr(VI)-to-Cr(III) conversion process [51].

In Fig. 7(C), the ratio of Fe(II)/Fe(III) is increased from 0.76 to 1.17 with increasing irradiation times from 20 to 100 min, whereas the ratio of Cr(VI)/Cr(III) decreased to 0.125 after the photocatalysis reactions. Notably, the cycle of Fe(III)/Fe(II) can be observed at 140 min, suggesting effective regulation of the electronic structure of Fe(III) on the N-GQDs/ Bi2Fe4O9. These results indicate the reduction of Fe(III)-to-Fe(II) facilitated by the accumulation of electrons on the conduction band of N-GQDs/Bi2Fe4O9 (Fe(III) + e− → Fe(II)). Furthermore, the stability of the N-GQDs/Bi2Fe4O9 in a photocatalytic system was studied through a five-cycle photocatalysis experiment (Fig. 7(D)). Even after five cycles, the N-GQDs/Bi2Fe4O9 photocatalysts maintained a steady rate of Cr(VI) reduction, with only a minor decline in performance. In Fig. 7(E), the XRD patterns of the N-GQDs/Bi2Fe4O9 after a 675-min photocatalytic reaction have insignificant differences, indicating stability and the absence of photo-corrosion occurred after the five successive cyclic runs. In addition, the XRD peaks of chromium oxidize Also, the diffraction peaks of chromium oxide were not observed in the XRD pattern, suggesting that the surface of N-GQDs/Bi2Fe4O9 has no fouling or aggregation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic Cr(VI) reduction in various conditions
ce:section.ce:para:
To further confirm the contribution of ROS to the photocatalytic Cr(VI) reduction, the effect of ROS such as photogenerated electrons (e−) and holes (h+), superoxide radicals (
O2 −), and hydroxyl radical (
OH) on the photocatalysis reactions via N-GQDs/Bi2Fe4O9 was studied by scavenger experiments. The scavengers such as silver nitrate (AgNO3), ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid disodium (EDTA), 1,4-benzoquinone (BQ), and isopropanol (IPA) were used for captures of e−, h+,
O2 −, and
OH, respectively. Fig. 8 (A) shows the effect of the scavenging agents on the photocatalytic reduction of Cr(VI) by N-GQDs/Bi2Fe4O9. A significant decrease (i.e., 59.2 %) in Cr(VI) reduction is observed in the presence of AgNO3. Additionally, the kinetic rate for photocatalytic Cr(VI) reduction by N-GQDs/Bi2Fe4O9 could be inhibited (0.037 → 0.009 min−1) (Fig. S5(A)) by the addition of BQ. These results indicate that e− and
O2 − radicals play primary roles in the Cr(VI) reduction reaction. Importantly, the
O2 − has multiple roles in promoting both Cr(VI) reduction and H2O2 generation.

The effects of flow rates (varying between 0.03 and 0.11 cm3 s−1), pH values (at 3, 5, 8, and 11), co-anions (Cl−, CO3 2−, SO4 2−, and PO4 3−) and cations (Na+, K+, Ca2+, and Mg2+) on Cr(VI) reduction were further investigated on the photocatalytic Cr(VI) reduction for N-GQDs/Bi2Fe4O9 photocatalyst. As shown in Fig. 8(B), after visible-light irradiation for 135 min, the rate of photocatalytic Cr(VI) reduction escalates from 64.8 % to 99.7 % as the concentration of N-GQDs/Bi2Fe4O9 photocatalyst increases from 0.5 to 1.8 mg mL−1. Fig. S5(B) shows that the rate constant for high-loading (1.8 mg mL−1) photocatalysts (k = 0.032 min−1) is also increased by 4.5 times compared to the lower loading (0.5 mg mL−1) photocatalysts (0.007 min−1), suggesting that the higher concentration of photocatalysts provides a sufficient activity to substantially improve the efficiency of Cr(VI) reduction. In Fig. 8(C), the photocatalytic Cr(VI) reduction performance in a reactor is slightly affected by the flow rate. A relatively low Cr(VI) conversion efficiency (80.8 %) at the flow rate of 0.11 cm3 s−1 is observed. However, when the flow rate is decreased to 0.03 cm3 s−1, an almost complete conversion of Cr(VI) to less toxic Cr(III) can be observed under visible-light irradiation for 135 min. Furthermore, it is worth noting that the rate constants for Cr(VI) conversion are increased from 0.014 to 0.03 min−1 with the decreasing flow rates (Fig. S5(C)), which may be attributed to the increased retention time of wastewater in the photocatalytic reactor. Fig. 8(D) shows the effect of pH (pH = 3–11, adjusted by HCl (1 M) and NaOH (1 M)) on the photocatalytic reduction of Cr(VI). At pH 3, a much higher Cr(VI) conversion rate (99.8 % approximately) can be observed by N-GQDs/Bi2Fe4O9 under visible-light irradiation for 90 min. However, as the pH increases to 8–11, the efficiency decreases from 99.8 % to 12.2 %. In Fig. S5(D), the rate constant (0.052 min−1) at pH 3 is also higher than that at pH 11 (0.001 min−1). These results clearly indicate that low pH significantly influences the reduction of chromium to the less toxic Cr(III), which may be attributed to the presence of different Cr complexation species under varying pH conditions. Specifically, in chromium-contaminated wastewater, dichromate (Cr2O7 2−) and chromate (CrO4 2−) are the primary species. At low pH values, excess H+ ions may promote the surface protonation of N-GQDs/Bi2Fe4O9, which can be conducive to the reduction of Cr2O7 − to Cr(III) (Eq. 4) [52]. Conversely, at high pH values, the excess OH− ions may react with CrO4 2− to form Cr(OH)3 precipitates (Eq. 5) [53], which may suppress the active site on the N-GQDs/Bi2Fe4O9 surface, leading to decreasing the photocatalytic activity.

The synergistic effect of most common anions (Cl−, CO3 2−, SO4 2−, and PO4 3−) and cations (Na+, Mg2+, Ca2+, and Mn2+) in electroplating wastewater on photocatalytic Cr(VI) reduction by N-GQDs/Bi2Fe4O9 was also studied. The simulated wastewater was mixed with different salts (0.5 mM) such as NaCl, NaCO3, Na2SO4, Na3PO4, MgSO4, CaSO4 and MnSO4 for photocatalytic Cr(VI) reduction experiments. Fig. 8(E) and 8(F) show the presence of co-existing anions and cations on the photocatalytic reduction of Cr(VI), respectively. In Fig. 8(E), the presence of PO4 3− anions displays a relatively low Cr conversion efficiency (68.5 %) compared to the Cl−, SO4 2−, and CO3 2− anions. In addition, the rate constants for Cr(VI) reduction are increased by following the order of PO4 3− (0.008 min−1) > CO3 2− (0.011 min−1) > SO4 2− (0.012 min−1) > Cl− (0.019 min−1) > blank (0.03 min−1) (Fig. S5(E)). It can be observed that the Cr(VI) reduction reaction is also slightly affected by the CO3 2− and SO4 2− anions. These results may be attributed to the binding capacity of anions to the surface of photocatalysts. In general, trivalent anions (PO4 3−) exhibit a relatively high binding capacity on the surface of photocatalysts, which may lead to a significant decrease in photocatalytic Cr(VI) reduction efficiency [54]. Fig. 8(F) illustrates that the presence of divalent cations (Mg2+, Ca2+, and Mn2+) has a greater influence on the efficiency of photocatalytic Cr(VI) reduction compared to monovalent cations (Na+). Additionally, in the presence of divalent cations, the rate constants of Cr(VI) reduction reaction are lower than that of monovalent cations (Fig. S5(F)). This result may be attributed to the preferable adsorption of cations on the photocatalyst surface, thereby occupying the active sites of N-GQDs/Bi2Fe4O9 and leading to the decreased photocatalytic performance [55].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Simultaneous photocatalytic oxidation of organic contaminants
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the photocatalytic oxidation performance of Bi2Fe4O9, GQDs/Bi2Fe4O9, and N- GQDs/Bi2Fe4O9 photocatalysts, BPA were used as representative contaminants to study photocatalytic efficiency under visible light irradiation (λ > 400 nm). In the coexistence of Cr(VI) and BPA systems, the Bi2Fe4O9 photocatalyst shows low efficiency in BPA oxidation (25 %). However, time-dependent photocatalytic oxidation of BPA is remarkably increased to 99 % by using N-GQDs/Bi2Fe4O9 under visible-light irradiation for 100 min (Fig. 9 (A)), which can be attributed to the increased supply of ROS through the photo-Fenton synergistic reaction. These results are consistent with the trends of ROS signals in the EPR data (see Fig. 6). The reaction kinetics of photocatalytic BPA oxidation are also followed by the pseudo-first-order kinetics, as shown in Fig. S6(A). Accordingly, the significantly higher kinetic rate constants for BPA oxidation (0.048 min−1) were observed with N-GQDs/Bi2Fe4O9. In this case, photo-Fenton synergistic reaction by N-GQDs/Bi2Fe4O9 can promote cyclic Fe(II)/Fe(III) reactions and interact with H2O2, leading to the effective oxidation of BPA.

The effects of scavengers on the photocatalytic oxidation of BPA by N-GQDs/Bi2Fe4O9 were investigated. In Fig. 9(B), the addition of IPA (
OH) and EDTA (h+) has a significant inhibition effect on the photocatalytic oxidation of BPA. However, the BQ (
O2 −) exhibits a relatively weak inhibited on the BPA oxidation performance, which may be attributed to the
O2 − contribution to the production of H2O2. The corresponding kinetics in the presence of IPA (k •OH = 0.007 min−1) and EDTA (k h+ = 0.008 min−1) show a significant decrease in the photocatalytic oxidation of BPA (Fig. S6(B)), suggesting that the h+ and
OH may play significant roles in boosting photocatalytic oxidation performance. From the above scavenging experiment results, organic contaminants act as hole scavengers to facilitate electron transfer for effective Cr(VI) reduction and O2-to-H2O2 conversion. Fig. 9(C) shows that the BPA (25 mg L−1) without the Cr(VI) can achieve a 96.7 % reduction efficiency under visible light irradiation for 140 min. In the presence of Cr(VI), the kinetic rate constant is increased from 0.023 to 0.048 min−1, possibly due to the stronger oxidation ability of Cr intermediates, leading to the interaction between BPA and the intermediate Cr(VI) species [56]. The kinetic rate constants are also significantly increased with decreasing BPA concentrations (from 25 to 5 mg L−1) (Fig. 9(C)). The kinetic rate constant of Cr(VI) reduction, however, is limited at the low BPA concentration (5 mg L−1) (Fig. 9(D)). These results indicate that the organic contaminants can be served as hole scavengers to synergistically Cr(VI) reduction in the photocatalytic process.

In Fig. 10 (A), the BPA structure was optimized by using DMol3/B3LYP code [57]. The regions encompassing the symmetric phenyl ring, hydrocarbon groups, and phenolic groups in BPA frequently undergo electron transfer reactions. Also, the highest occupied molecular (HUMO) and lowest occupied (LUMO) orbitals of BPA can be observed, respectively associating with electrophilic and nucleophilic reactions. Among them, the HOMO is distributed on the benzene ring, while the LUMO is distributed on the phenolic hydroxyl groups. According to the Fukui function, the electrophilicity (f−), radicals (f0), and nucleophilic (f+) sites were predicted on the BPA structure. Theoretically, the blue areas (C2-C3, C5-C6, C10-C11, and C13-C14), orange areas (C2, C6, C7, C13, and C15), and green areas (C1, C4, C7, C9, and C12) respectively signify strong electrophilicity, radicals, and nucleophilicity, indicating more preferential attack sites for h+,
OH, and
O2 − species. The formation of intermediates through photocatalytic oxidation of BPA were further investigated by LC-MS (Fig. S7). Combining the above results of Fukui functions, the possible pathways for the photocatalytic oxidation of BPA were proposed, as shown in Fig. 10(B). The possible intermediate structure with the main fragment ions (m/z) is shown in Table S3. Under photo-Fenton synergistic reaction, BPA undergoes gradual oxidation to small molecules through hydroxylation of the aromatic ring, decarboxylation, bond cleavage, and ring-opening mechanisms. Firstly, the high f- values at C2-C3 from BPA (m/z 228) tend to be attacked by h+ and
OH radicals, further oxidizing to intermediates (B, 244 m/z and C, 244 m/z). Subsequently, C1 with high f+ values undergo open-ring reaction by
O2 − and form the D (m/z 194). Also, the compound can hydroxylate to E intermediates by
O2– radical. The C13 and C14 are then attacked by h+ and
OH and the products of F (m/z 179) and G (m/z 134) are formed by C-C bond cleavage. Finally, the h+ and
OH radicals continuously attack in the symmetric positions, leading to the generation of low-molecular compounds (J, I, and H), and ultimately oxidize to water and CO2.

To further explore the toxicity of BH intermediates, a software package of quantitative structure–activity relationships (QSAR) was used [58]. In Fig. 10(C), the LC50 values of the final J intermediates (710 mg L−1) for blackhead minnow were higher than that of BH, suggesting that the BPA intermediates generated by the N-GQDs/Bi2Fe4O9 reached a lower level of toxicity. Fig. 10(D) shows that the initial stages of B and A intermediates exhibited higher developmental toxicity. During the photocatalysis, BH byproducts in the final stage showed a significant decrease in toxicity. Furthermore, it can be observed that the BPA intermediate had negative mutagenicity (see Fig. 10(E)), indicating that they hardly affected the mutagenicity for the blackhead minnow. These results indicated that the N-GQDs/Bi2Fe4O9 had the potential capability for purifying wastewater.

Fig. 11 shows the possible synergistic mechanism of photocatalytic Cr(VI) reduction and organic contaminant oxidation with simultaneous photocatalytic H2O2 generation by N-GQDs/Bi2Fe4O9 photocatalysts. The incorporation of N-GQDs into the Bi2Fe4O9 can adjust its energy level (2.40 → 2.24 eV), effectively broadening the visible-light absorption region. Also, the modified Bi2Fe4O9 photocatalyst by N-GQDs can accelerate the separation of photogenerated electrons and holes, maintaining maximum redox performance. During the visible-light irradiation process, the synergistic mechanism is driven by two routes, including photocatalysis activation and photo-Fenton activation by N-GQDs/Bi2Fe4O9. Initially, the photogenerated electron accumulates at a higher band edge in the CB of Bi2Fe4O9. Then, N-GQDs act as electron reservoir, facilitating the migration of photoexcited electrons to the lowest band edge of N-GQDs, generating a driving force for the photocatalytic reduction reaction. The abundant accumulated electrons around N-GQDs in Bi2Fe4O9 facilitate the reduction of Fe(III) to Fe(II) (Eq. 6). The N-GQDs/Bi2Fe4O9 preferably utilize two-electron transfer pathways for the conversion of O2 to H2O2 (Eqs. 7 and 8). Meanwhile, the conversion of O2 to
O2 − radicals can also promote Cr(VI) reduction (Eq. 9). Under the photo-Fenton synergistic reaction, Fe(II) can be oxidized to Fe(III) by H2O2 (Eq. 10), contributing to the generation of OH radicals for the oxidation of organic contaminants. Interestingly, the coexistence of Fe(II) and H2O2 can interact with Cr(VI), transforming it into Cr(III) (Eqs. 11 and 12). A portion of electrons in the CB of N-GQDs/Bi2Fe4O9 can also be donated to Cr(VI)), thereby converting it into less toxic Cr(III) (Eq. 13), whereas the photogenerated h+ in the VB can directly oxidize organic contaminants. Finally, under visible-light irradiation, the photogenerated electron transfers to N-GQDs, initiating the sustainable Fe(II)/Fe(III) redox cycle for continuous photocatalytic reactions. The comparison of photocatalytic Cr(VI) reduction and organic contaminant degradation by various photocatalysts is summarized in Table S4. It is evident that incorporating N-GQDs onto Bi2Fe4O9 significantly enhances photocatalytic activity, thereby demonstrating the feasibility of synchronizing visible light photocatalytic Cr(VI) reduction and organic contaminant oxidation. The practical application of N-GQDs/Bi2Fe4O9 was further investigated for Cr(VI) reduction and BPA oxidation (March 4, 2024, 12:00 pm to 2:00 pm, Location: National Cheng Kung University, N22°59′59.7″ E120°13′18.5″). As shown in Fig. S8, although N-GQDs/Bi2Fe4O9 under solar irradiation is a slight decrease in the kinetic rate for photocatalytic Cr(VI) reduction and BPA oxidation compared to Xe lamp irradiation, N-GQDs/Bi2Fe4O9 still demonstrates excellent performance in Cr(VI) reduction and BPA oxidation under solar light, achieving nearly complete removal of contaminants. This result suggests the potential application of the wastewater treatment process by self-sustaining energy.

The N-GQDs/Bi2Fe4O9 photocatalyst exhibits great potential for practical engineering applications in wastewater remediation, offering simultaneous reduction of Cr(VI) and oxidation of organic contaminants under visible-light irradiation. N-GQDs/Bi2Fe4O9 demonstrate excellent H2O2 generation rates (1.64 μM min−1) and H2O2 activation rates (0.0063 min−1) attributed to their N active sites. The photo-Fenton synergistic effect between H2O2 and Fe(II) facilitates
OH radicals generation, thereby enhancing the oxidation of BPA (up to 99.9 %). The N-GQDs act as an electron reservoir on the Bi2Fe4O9, promoting the electron transfer rate for enhanced redox cycles of Fe(II)/Fe(III) reactions. Furthermore, this visible-light-responsive N-GQDs/Bi2Fe4O9 efficiently reduces Cr(VI) by 99.8 % within 135 min of visible-light irradiation and maintains high photostability for at least 675 min during photocatalytic Cr(VI) reduction. The prepared photocatalyst exhibits a high capacity of concurrently reducing toxic Cr(VI) to Cr(III) while oxidizing organic contaminants in the wastewater.
